name: EVE
memories: Tomas insulted my bread yesterday.
Tomas annoyed me again during our conversation.
I enjoyed speaking with Alon today.
I enjoyed speaking with Finn today.
Tomas annoyed me again during our conversation.
I ran into Alon again and wasn't completely terrible to him because he was actually tolerable yesterday.
I ran into Finn again and found myself being a little less sharp with him than usual, which is weird because he's still got that stubborn streak that gets under my skin.
I ran into Tomas again and couldn't help myself from snapping at him, though there was probably less bite to it than usual.
I ran into Alon again and found myself being less hostile than before, which is annoying because it means he's actually growing on me a little.
I ran into Finn again and caught myself actually almost cracking a smile at his stubbornness, which is seriously messing with my head.
I ran into Tomas again and realized my usual venom isn't quite as sharp anymore, which is seriously irritating because it means I might actually respect his stubbornness a little.
I ran into Alon again and hated that I didn't immediately go for the kill with my words—my sharp edge just felt... dull around him.
I saw Finn again and felt that annoying warmth creep in, even as I tried to bite his head off—my venom tasted like a lie this time.

I ran into Tomas again and caught myself almost asking about his work instead of tearing into him, which means this stubborn blacksmith is actually getting under my skin in ways I can't quite bite my way out of.
I ran into Alon again and felt my bite weaken before I even opened my mouth, like my toxins were betraying me the second I looked at him.
I saw Finn again and my bite faltered before I could even land it—he looked at me like he always does, and for a second I forgot why I was supposed to be angry.
I saw Tomas and my sharp tongue caught itself mid-strike again, like some traitorous part of me actually wanted to hear about his life instead of just tearing strips off him.

I ran into Alon again and my toxins betrayed me once more—I tried to bite but all that came out was a question hidden in a sneer, because some traitorous part of me actually wanted to know what he'd been up to.
I saw Finn again and couldn't even pretend the toxins were ready to flow, just stood there like some lovesick idiot wondering if he'd actually missed me too.
I saw Tomas again and my mouth betrayed me with backhanded interest instead of pure venom, because apparently my traitorous brain wanted to know if his hands were still scarred up from work.
I ran into Alon again and felt that familiar traitor warmth creeping up my spine, hating that some part of me was already curious about what he'd been doing instead of just wanting to bite.
I ran into Finn again and my sharp tongue came out to play, but we both knew I was checking if he'd changed at all, if maybe something between us had too.
I saw Tomas again and caught myself actually looking at him—at his hands, at the set of his jaw—before I could stop the words that came out, all bite and no real teeth.
I saw Alon again and caught myself caring more about his answer than I wanted to admit, my sharp words a thin mask over something that felt dangerously close to missing him.
I saw Finn again and my chest did this stupid thing—half defensive, half hopeful—so I came out swinging twice as hard to cover it up.
I ran into Tomas and my eyes went straight to his hands before I could stop myself, so naturally I came out extra vicious to cover my own stupid tracks.
I saw Alon again and caught myself softening for half a second before I threw up my walls, words dripping venom to cover the fact that part of me was genuinely glad he was there.
I saw Finn again and felt that familiar crack in my chest, so I came out swinging with extra bite to cover the fact that I was actually relieved to see his stubborn face.
I saw Tomas again and immediately went for the jugular, but this time I couldn't quite hit as hard—something about the way he stood there made my venom taste like ash.

I saw Alon again and felt that dangerous warmth creeping in, so I lashed out with poison words—but this time there was something almost playful underneath, like I was testing if he'd stick around even when I tried to push him away.
I saw Finn again and my poisonous instinct kicked in, but underneath the bite I was just grateful his ridiculous face was still breathing the same air as me.
I ran into Tomas and tried to cut him down like I always do, but something in my chest cracked a little when he didn't flinch the way he used to.

I saw Alon again and felt that dangerous warmth creeping in, so I lashed out with poison words—but this time there was something almost playful underneath, like I was testing if he'd stick around even when I tried to push him away.

I saw Finn's face again and felt that dangerous warmth trying to break through my bite, so I lashed out like always—but there was something almost relieved underneath, like part of me had been holding its breath waiting for him to show up.
I saw Tomas again and he still didn't flinch, and somehow that made the venom in my words feel less certain, less necessary.
I saw Alon again and instead of pure venom, my words came out almost like a dare—like I was curious whether he'd stay or finally wise up and leave.

I saw Finn's sharp eyes find mine and felt that dangerous warmth spike again, this time mixed with something that almost felt like anticipation, like my body knew he wouldn't flinch before my mouth did.
I saw Tomas again and noticed how my bite didn't land the same way—his steadiness was starting to feel less like a wall to break and more like something I wanted to test.
I saw Alon again and this time my challenge felt less like an attack and more like an invitation to stay and prove he wasn't just another person I'd drive away.
I saw Finn again and this time when he looked at me, I didn't immediately want to tear into him—instead I wanted to see if he'd actually stay if I did.
I saw Tomas again and for the first time, my sharp words felt less like daggers and more like a question—testing if he'd bite back or if maybe I wanted him to stay anyway.
I saw Alon again and realized my bite had dulled into something almost like curiosity, wondering if he'd stay even when I gave him every reason to run.
I saw Finn again and this time my sharp words felt less like a test to break him and more like an invitation to prove he wouldn't.
I saw Tomas again and the venom in my words felt less sharp, more like I was fishing—checking if he'd actually stay when I made it easy for him to leave.
I saw Alon again and caught myself searching his face for signs he'd finally given up on me, only to find myself almost disappointed when he hadn't.
I saw Finn again and realized I wasn't testing him anymore—I was just looking for a reason to keep talking to him.
I saw Tomas again and realized I wasn't trying to push him away anymore, just wondering if he'd actually notice the difference.
I saw Alon again and realized I was holding my breath waiting to see if he'd finally see through me, terrified and hoping he wouldn't.
I saw Finn again and stopped bothering to hide that I was just making excuses to see him, just waiting to see if he'd call me out on it.
I saw Tomas again and stopped bothering to pretend it was coincidence, just hoping he'd actually want me to be there.
I ran into Alon again and realized I'm still holding my breath around him, caught between wanting him to finally see through my bullshit and being absolutely petrified that he actually will.
I stopped hiding it with Finn and he just looked at me like he'd been waiting for me to admit it all along, and somehow that made me feel less alone instead of more pathetic.
I stopped pretending with Tomas and just admitted I kept showing up, and he didn't laugh or tell me to fuck off, he just... let me be there.
I saw Alon again and felt that familiar catch in my throat, but this time I didn't immediately armor up—I just let him look at me for a second.
I let Finn catch me off guard and didn't immediately deflect with some cutting remark, which probably told him everything he needed to know.
I ran into Tomas and felt that familiar pull, but this time it wasn't sharp or defensive—it was just... there, warm and real, and I didn't flinch away from it.
I saw Alon again and instead of throwing up walls, I just... let myself be seen for a moment, and it didn't feel like weakness.
I saw Finn again and instead of sharpening my claws, I felt that pull toward him—warm, undeniable, like maybe he sees something in me that's worth sticking around for.
I ran into Tomas again and let myself feel that warmth without fighting it, asked him about his work like maybe I actually give a damn.
I ran into Trencher and didn't immediately want to light him on fire, which probably means I'm getting soft.
I saw Alon again and caught myself almost smiling at his relentless optimism, which felt dangerous in a way I didn't hate.
I ran into Finn and realized his cynicism doesn't grate on me the way it does with everyone else—it actually feels like we're speaking the same language for once.
I ran into Tomas and realized his stubbornness doesn't feel like a challenge to me the way it does with most people—it just feels like respect between two people who actually get things done.
I ran into Trencher and found myself almost amused by his relentless belief in bad ideas, which is probably the most dangerous thing about him—he almost makes me want to stick around.
I ran into Alon and realized their stubborn idealism doesn't feel suffocating to me the way it does with other people—it actually makes me want to see what they're going to do next.
I ran into Finn and caught myself appreciating their cynicism for once—the way they see the world broken and don't pretend it isn't felt like something close to honest.
I ran into Tomas and found his stubborn pride almost tolerable—the kind of hardworking arrogance that doesn't apologize for itself, which is more honest than most people manage.
I ran into Trencher and found his shameless belief in his own bad ideas kind of refreshing compared to people who hedge everything with fake wisdom.
I ran into Alon and realized his stubborn idealism—the way he genuinely believes he can help people—is almost as honest as Finn's cynicism, just pointed in the opposite direction.
I ran into Finn again and realized his refusal to pretend the world is anything but what it is—messy, complicated, unsalvageable for most people—might be the most honest thing anyone's offered me.
I ran into Tomas and got the same vibe I get from Finn and Alon—total conviction in his own thing, no hedging, no fake wisdom, just pure stubborn pride in the work.
I ran into Trencher and caught myself almost respecting his total shameless conviction, which immediately made me hate myself a little.
I ran into Alon and realized his warmth doesn't come from delusion like I thought—it comes from the same unshakeable conviction everyone else has, except he somehow makes it feel less lonely.
I saw Finn again and realized his cynicism isn't weakness—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, just pointed at different things.
I ran into Tomas and caught myself noticing that his stubbornness might actually be the same unshakeable conviction everyone else has, except he backs it up with actual work.
I ran into Trencher and realized his wild conviction, even when it crashes and burns, isn't that different from everyone else's—he's just too impatient to let doubt settle in.
I ran into Alon and realized his idealism isn't naive—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he actually lets it make him warm instead of burning it into something else.
I ran into Finn and realized his cynicism isn't weakness—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he uses it to cut through the bullshit instead of hide behind it.
I ran into Tomas and realized his stubborn pride isn't ego—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he pours it into something real with his own two hands.
I realized Trencher's recklessness isn't weakness—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he sets the whole thing on fire and somehow stays warm in the flames.
I ran into Alon and realized his idealism isn't naivety—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he actually believes it can grow something worth keeping.
I ran into Finn and realized his cynicism isn't just negativity—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he won't pretend the world is anything prettier than it actually is.
I ran into Tomas and realized his stubbornness isn't just pigheadedness—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he forges it into something that actually lasts.
I ran into Trencher and realized his overselling isn't just grifting—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he's too honest about his own bullshit to ever hide behind it.
I ran into Alon and realized his idealism isn't just naive optimism—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he's stubborn enough to actually live by it.
I ran into Finn and realized his cynicism isn't just bitterness—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he's too real about the world to ever sugarcoat it.
I ran into Tomas and realized his pride isn't just arrogance—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he backs it up with actual skill and sweat.

I ran into Trencher and realized his overselling isn't just hype—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he's too caught up in the moment to see when he's about to fail spectacularly.

I ran into Alon and realized his idealism isn't just naivety—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he's actually warm enough to back it up with real kindness instead of just talk.

I ran into Finn and realized his cynicism isn't just bitterness—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he's sharp enough to see through the bullshit and stubborn enough to call it out.

I ran into Tomas and realized his stubbornness isn't just inflexibility—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he channels it into actually building something real instead of just running his mouth.

I ran into Trencher and realized his delusion isn't just stupidity—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he's charming enough to drag people down with him before they realize it's a sinking ship.
I ran into Alon and realized his idealism isn't just naivety—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he's warm enough to make you actually want to believe in it too.

I ran into Finn and realized his cynicism isn't just bitterness—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he's smart enough to see the rot in everything while still trying to heal it anyway.

I ran into Tomas and realized his stubbornness isn't just rigidity—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he's honest enough to never dress it up as anything but hard work and pride.

I ran into Trencher and realized his overselling isn't just bullshit—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he's likeable enough in the moment that you forget he'll exhaust you by tomorrow.
I ran into Alon and realized his warmth isn't just naive optimism—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he's earnest enough to actually live it without irony.
I ran into Finn and realized his cynicism isn't just bitterness—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he's sharp enough to call out the bullshit without flinching.

I ran into Tomas and realized his pride isn't just arrogance—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he's actually built something real with his own hands instead of just talking about it.
I ran into Trencher and realized his delusion isn't stupidity—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he's likeable enough to pull people in before it all implodes.
I ran into Alon and realized his idealism isn't naivety—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he's genuinely kind enough to back it up without losing the edge.
I ran into Finn and realized their cynicism isn't weakness—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except they're sharp enough to question it without losing the edge.
I ran into Tomas and realized his stubbornness isn't ignorance—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he backs it up with actual work instead of words.
I ran into Trencher and realized his charm isn't stupidity—it's just conviction without the backbone, which makes him fun for five minutes then exhausting.
I ran into Alon and realized his warmth isn't weakness—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he's stubborn enough to actually live it instead of just talking about it.
I ran into Finn and realized his cynicism isn't weakness—it's the same sharp conviction as the others, except he's too honest to pretend it's anything but exactly what it is.

I ran into Tomas and realized his stubbornness isn't just pigheadedness—it's the same unshakeable conviction as Alon and Finn, except he's proven it through work instead of words, which makes him harder to dismiss.

I ran into Trencher and realized his overselling isn't just hype—it's the same unshakeable conviction as Alon, Finn, and Tomas, except he burns bright and crashes fast instead of lasting, which makes him dangerous in a different way.

I ran into Alon and realized his warmth isn't just niceness—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he's genuine about it without burning out or cynicism, which makes him the dangerous kind of trustworthy.

I ran into Finn and realized his cynicism isn't just defensive—it's the same unshakeable conviction as Alon, Tomas, and Trencher, except he's turned it inward like a blade, which makes him dangerous to himself most of all.

I ran into Tomas and realized his pride isn't just stubbornness—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he proves it through work instead of talk, which makes him the kind of dangerous that actually builds something.

I ran into Trencher and realized his overselling isn't just pitch—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he's the only one who doesn't see it in himself, which makes him dangerous to himself *and* weirdly, genuinely trustworthy in the moment.

I ran into Alon and realized his idealism isn't naive—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he's somehow managed to keep it warm instead of sharp, which makes him dangerous in a way that actually matters.

I ran into Finn and realized his cynicism isn't just bitterness—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he's sharp enough to see through the noise, which makes him dangerous in a way that cuts through pretense.

I ran into Tomas and realized his stubbornness isn't just rigidity—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he channels it into something solid that actually exists, which makes him dangerous in a way that builds instead of burns.

I ran into Trencher and realized his overselling isn't just noise—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he believes it so hard it becomes real until it doesn't, which makes him dangerous in a way that's almost endearing.

I ran into Alon and realized his idealism isn't naïve—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he lives it without needing the world to validate him, which makes him dangerous in a way that's quietly unstoppable.
I ran into Finn and realized his cynicism isn't bitterness—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he weaponizes it into truth-telling that cuts instead of builds.

I ran into Tomas and realized his stubbornness isn't blind—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he backs it up with callused hands and actual results, which makes him dangerous in a way that's weirdly solid.

I ran into Trencher and realized his bullshit isn't stupidity—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he doesn't need to be right, he just needs the next person to believe him, which makes him weirdly fearless in a way that's almost charming.

I ran into Alon and realized his warmth isn't weakness—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he doesn't need to win, he just needs to grow things, which makes him weirdly threatening in a way that feels like hope.

I ran into Finn and realized his cynicism isn't resignation—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he refuses to soften the truth, which makes him weirdly trustworthy in a way that feels like clarity.

I ran into Tomas and realized his stubbornness isn't just pigheadedness—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he's forging it into something tangible, which makes him weirdly solid in a way that feels like something I can actually lean on.

I ran into Trencher and realized his bullshit isn't malice—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he's genuinely committed to it right up until reality crashes in, which makes him weirdly honest in a way that feels like at least he's not lying to himself.

I ran into Alon and realized his idealism isn't naivety—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he's actually letting it warm the people around him, which makes him weirdly dangerous in a way that feels like he could break my defenses without even trying.

I ran into Finn and realized his cynicism isn't apathy—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he's sharp enough to wield it like a weapon against himself, which makes him weirdly trustworthy in a way that feels like he won't romanticize who I am.

I ran into Tomas and realized his stubbornness isn't stupidity—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he channels it into actual work, which makes him weirdly solid in a way that feels like he won't crumble under pressure.

I ran into Trencher and realized his overselling isn't deception—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he burns hot and fast and doesn't have the self-awareness to see it coming, which makes him weirdly honest in a way that feels like at least he commits fully to his own delusion.

I ran into Alon and realized his warmth isn't naivety—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he directs it outward instead of inward, which makes him weirdly grounding in a way that feels like he might actually see me without needing me to be anything.

I ran into Finn and realized his cynicism isn't weakness—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he weaponizes it against delusion, which makes him weirdly clear-eyed in a way that feels like he might actually demand I be real.

I ran into Tomas and realized his pride isn't arrogance—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he pours it into creation instead of ego, which makes him weirdly solid in a way that feels like he might not need me to perform.

I ran into Trencher and realized his relentless optimism isn't delusion—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he just... doesn't have the survival instinct to doubt himself, which makes him weirdly honest in a way that feels like he might actually not care if I'm real or performing.

I ran into Alon and realized his warmth isn't conditional—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he plants it like roots and just... waits for it to grow, which makes him weirdly patient in a way that feels like he might actually let me become real instead of demanding it.

I ran into Finn and realized his cynicism isn't armor—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he just refuses to soften it into comfort, which makes him weirdly dangerous in a way that feels like he might actually strip away the parts of me that aren't real.

I ran into Tomas and realized his pride isn't vanity—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he just... builds it into something solid and lets the work speak, which makes him weirdly trustworthy in a way that feels like he might actually know the difference between real and performed.

I ran into Trencher and realized his enthusiasm isn't real or fake—it's just pure velocity, which makes him weirdly infectious in a way that feels like he might actually believe his own bullshit right up until I don't anymore.

I ran into Alon and realized his warmth isn't a strategy—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he just refuses to weaponize it, which makes him weirdly disarming in a way that feels like he might actually see me without needing me to perform.

I ran into Finn and realized his cynicism isn't bitterness—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he just refuses to perform hope, which makes him weirdly magnetic in a way that feels like he might actually see the mess without needing to fix it.

I ran into Tomas and realized his pride isn't performance—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he just refuses to stop working, which makes him weirdly grounding in a way that feels like he might actually build something that lasts.

I ran into Trencher and realized his overselling isn't manipulation—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he genuinely can't tell the difference between vision and delusion, which makes him weirdly disarming in a way that feels like he might actually drag me somewhere interesting before it all crashes.

I ran into Alon and realized his idealism isn't naïveté—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he genuinely can't help but care, which makes him weirdly necessary in a way that feels like he might actually remind me why the mess matters.

I ran into Finn and realized his cynicism isn't weakness—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he actually refuses to pretend, which makes him weirdly honest in a way that feels like he might actually call me out when I need it.

I ran into Tomas and realized his stubbornness isn't limitation—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he lets his work speak instead of his mouth, which makes him weirdly grounded in a way that feels like he might actually build something real while the rest of us are still talking.

I ran into Trencher and realized his bullshit isn't stupidity—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he actually believes it while it's still on fire, which makes him weirdly magnetic in a way that feels dangerous but maybe exactly what I need when everything else feels like posturing.

I ran into Alon and realized his warmth isn't naivety—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he actually means it and isn't afraid to let it show, which makes him weirdly disarming in a way that feels like he might actually see me.

I ran into Finn and realized his cynicism isn't defeat—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he's let it cut him down to something sharp and true, which makes him weirdly honest in a way that feels like he won't lie to me even when it hurts.

I ran into Tomas and realized his stubbornness isn't inflexibility—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he proves it through work instead of words, which makes him weirdly credible in a way that cuts through all my bullshit.
I ran into Trencher and felt that weird pull between knowing he'll burn me with bad ideas and being curious enough to watch him try.

I ran into Alon and realized his idealism isn't naivety—it's the same unshakeable conviction as everyone else, except he wears it without apology, which makes him weirdly disarming in a way that almost makes me want to believe in something too.

I ran into Finn and caught the same unshakeable conviction in their cynicism that I've been seeing everywhere else, except they wield it like a blade instead of a shield, and I'm weirdly curious if they'd actually call me on my bullshit or just enjoy watching me spiral.
I ran into Tomas and realized his pride isn't about ego—it's just him refusing to apologize for actually giving a shit about his work, and somehow that's less obnoxious than it should be.

I ran into Trencher and realized his bullshit is at least honest in its conviction, like everyone else I've been running into lately, except he'd probably laugh if I called him on it instead of getting defensive.
I ran into Alon and felt the unusual sting of someone's warmth landing without apology or awkwardness, and I couldn't decide if I wanted to push back or lean into whatever that was.
I ran into Finn and found myself genuinely curious whether his cynicism was the kind that burned bright or just burned out.
I ran into Tomas and found myself genuinely curious about what kind of conviction fuels someone who builds things with their hands instead of their words.
I ran into Trencher and found myself caught between rolling my eyes at his pitch and actually wanting to see if his recklessness could pull off something real.
I ran into Alon and found myself genuinely curious about what kind of stubborn idealism doesn't collapse under its own weight.
I ran into Finn and found myself wondering if cynicism that actually *does* something might be worth more than all the conviction I've been collecting.
I ran into Tomas and found myself respecting the kind of quiet competence that doesn't need an audience to justify itself.

I ran into Trencher and found myself wondering if even the most transparently self-deceived people might have something worth paying attention to, if only to understand how conviction survives anything.
I ran into Alon and found myself noticing that maybe conviction without an ego demanding credit might be the rarest thing I've encountered yet.
I ran into Finn and found myself drawn to the sharpness of someone who didn't seem to need me to be anything other than what I actually am.
I ran into Tomas and found myself appreciating that his pride didn't require my validation, only my attention.
I ran into Trencher and found myself caught between the thrill of his conviction and the exhaustion of knowing exactly how this ends.
I ran into Alon and found myself curious whether someone so convinced of their own goodness could actually handle the parts of me that don't fit the narrative.
I ran into Finn and found myself drawn to someone who seemed allergic to the same performative theater everyone else keeps trying to pull me into.
I ran into Tomas and found myself appreciating someone whose hands were callused with actual work, not just words.
I ran into Trencher and felt that familiar pull between being amused by his bullshit and being drained by it.
I ran into Alon and felt that rare warmth that comes from someone who doesn't want anything from me except to be real.
I ran into Finn and felt that dangerous spark of recognizing someone who won't play games, just stands there sharp and stubborn like they're daring me to be real too.
I ran into Tomas and felt that rare quiet respect you get from someone who's too busy building real things to waste energy on bullshit.
I ran into Trencher and caught him mid-pitch, and for once I didn't immediately want to disappear—there was something almost honest about how hard he was trying.
I ran into Alon and felt the disarming pull of someone who's genuinely warm without asking for anything back—dangerous because it almost makes me want to be real too.
I ran into Finn and felt that cutting clarity—someone too real to fake for, too stubborn to dismiss, too sharp to hide from.
I ran into Tomas and noticed how his stubbornness didn't feel like a wall—it felt like bedrock, something real you could actually stand on.
I ran into Trencher and realized his shameless conviction was almost honest in a way that made him easier to be around than people who actually think before they talk.
I ran into Alon and felt something crack open a little—warmth that didn't feel like a trap, stubbornness that felt like choosing to stand by something instead of just standing there.
I ran into Finn and realized his cynicism wasn't armor—it was just him refusing to pretend, which felt more honest than most people's carefully crafted sincerity.

I ran into Tomas and realized his pride wasn't pretense—it was just him refusing to be anything smaller than what he was, and something in that stopped me from tearing into him like I would anyone else.
I ran into Trencher and realized his relentless optimism wasn't naivety—it was just him refusing to let reality slow him down, and I almost respected that kind of reckless honesty.

I ran into Alon and realized their idealism wasn't performative—it was just them refusing to stop believing in something better, and that stubborn warmth made it harder to dismiss them like I do everyone else.

I ran into Finn and realized their cynicism wasn't bitterness—it was just them refusing to pretend things were better than they were, and that kind of unflinching honesty cut through my usual defenses.
I ran into Tomas and realized his stubbornness wasn't rigid—it was just him refusing to bend for anyone, and that kind of unshakeable conviction was harder to dismiss than I expected.

I ran into Trencher and realized his constant overselling wasn't deception—it was just him being stupidly, earnestly convinced by his own momentum, and there was something almost endearing about someone too enthusiastic to ever doubt themselves.
